title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about gardens and plants. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: What the Garden Knows
content.subtitle: Gardens, culture, and the intelligence of plant life
content.text:
The garden has occupied a peculiar place in human culture for thousands of years — neither fully wild nor fully built, neither natural nor artificial, but suspended between the two. [31]Historians of landscape have described the garden as a space in which human beings have always enacted their deepest assumptions about nature, beauty, order, and the proper relationship between people and the natural world.[/31]

The form a garden takes tells us something about the culture that produced it. [32]The formal gardens of seventeenth-century France, with their geometrically precise hedges and mathematically ordered paths, expressed a Cartesian ideal of nature brought under rational control. The English landscape garden of the following century, with its studied irregularity and artfully positioned trees, rejected this formalism in favour of an idealised version of nature that was, paradoxically, as carefully constructed as anything it replaced.[/32]

In recent decades, the garden has become a site of ecological concern as well as aesthetic pleasure. [33]The rise of the wildlife garden — featuring native plants, wildflower meadows, and habitats for insects and small animals — reflects a shift in values from the garden as controlled display to the garden as active contributor to local biodiversity.[/33] For many gardeners, this shift has involved not only practical changes but a fundamental reorientation of purpose.

Science has added another dimension to our understanding of plants and gardens. [34]Research in plant biology over the past two decades has revealed that plants are far more responsive to their environment than was previously understood — capable of chemical communication with neighbouring plants, of distinguishing between different types of mechanical contact, and of altering their growth patterns in response to specific environmental pressures.[/34]

These findings have prompted a reassessment of the way we think about plant experience. [35]The philosopher and botanist Michael Marder has argued that plants possess a form of intelligence that is fundamentally different from animal intelligence — distributed, non-centralised, and oriented not toward individual survival but toward a kind of responsive engagement with the world.[/35]

[36]The garden, perhaps, is the place where these philosophical questions become most tangible. In tending a garden, we cannot avoid confronting the irreducible otherness of plant life — a form of being that is patient, persistent, and ultimately indifferent to our interpretations of it.[/36]

questions.question: How do historians of landscape describe the significance of gardens?
options: A) As spaces that have gradually replaced wilderness in many parts of the world.
B) As environments used mainly for the cultivation of food throughout human history.
C) As places designed primarily for rest and recreation across different cultures.
D) As spaces that reveal a culture's fundamental beliefs about nature and humanity's place within it.
questions.explanation: Historians describe gardens as spaces where people have 'enacted their deepest assumptions about nature, beauty, order, and the proper relationship between people and the natural world.'

questions.question: What does the contrast between French and English garden styles illustrate?
options: A) That different cultures and periods express different assumptions about the ideal relationship with nature.
B) That formal garden design is generally preferred by cultures with a strong mathematical tradition.
C) That English gardens are more natural and less deliberately designed than their French counterparts.
D) That garden design has consistently moved toward greater informality over the centuries.
questions.explanation: French gardens express rational control of nature while English ones embody a contrasting ideal, showing how garden style reflects cultural assumptions about nature.

questions.question: What change in values does the wildlife garden represent?
options: A) A rejection of the idea that gardens can be aesthetically pleasing environments.
B) A belief that private gardens should all be converted into public parks.
C) A shift from seeing the garden as a controlled display to seeing it as contributing to the environment.
D) A recognition that traditional gardening practices are too costly and time-consuming.
questions.explanation: The text describes 'a shift in values from the garden as controlled display to the garden as active contributor to local biodiversity.'

questions.question: What has recent scientific research revealed about plants?
options: A) That certain species of plant are capable of basic movement in response to light and gravity.
B) That they interact with and respond to their surroundings in far more sophisticated ways than was known.
C) That plants can store and retrieve detailed information about past environmental conditions.
D) That some plant species communicate in ways closely resembling simple animal behaviour.
questions.explanation: Research shows plants are 'far more responsive to their environment than was previously understood,' including chemical communication and adaptive growth responses.

questions.question: What is distinctive about the form of intelligence Michael Marder attributes to plants?
options: A) It resembles animal intelligence but operates at a much slower pace.
B) It is centralised in specific organs that function like a simple brain.
C) It is primarily concerned with the reproduction and spread of the species.
D) It is distributed throughout the organism and focused on interaction with the environment.
questions.explanation: Marder describes plant intelligence as 'distributed, non-centralised, and oriented toward a kind of responsive engagement with the world,' contrasting with animal intelligence.

questions.question: What does the final paragraph suggest about the experience of tending a garden?
options: A) It brings people into direct contact with a form of life that cannot be fully understood on human terms.
B) It is the most effective way for people to develop a practical understanding of plant biology.
C) It offers a more meaningful relationship with nature than any other human activity can provide.
D) It has become increasingly important as a response to the widespread loss of natural habitats.
questions.explanation: Tending a garden means 'confronting the irreducible otherness of plant life — a form of being that is patient, persistent, and ultimately indifferent to our interpretations of it.'
